key_points: Drainage refers to the river system of an area.
A drainage basin is the area drained by a river and its tributaries.
A water divide is an upland that separates two drainage basins.
India's drainage system is mainly divided into Himalayan rivers and Peninsular rivers.
Himalayan rivers originate from glaciers and are perennial.
Peninsular rivers originate from the plateau and mainly depend on rainfall.
The three major Himalayan river systems are Indus, Ganga and Brahmaputra.
The Sundarban delta formed by the Ganga and Brahmaputra is the largest delta in the world.
Narmada and Tapi are the major west-flowing rivers of Peninsular India.
Most Peninsular rivers flow eastwards into the Bay of Bengal.
examples: Example of west flowing river: Narmada
Largest freshwater lake in India: Wular Lake
formulas: Drainage = River system of an area
Drainage basin = Area drained by a river and its tributaries
Water divide = Upland separating two drainage basins
Perennial rivers = Rivers that flow throughout the year
exam_tips: Remember the two main river systems: Himalayan rivers and Peninsular rivers.
Understand the sources and courses of the Indus, Ganga and Brahmaputra rivers.
Focus on differences between Himalayan and Peninsular rivers.
Learn the importance of rivers for irrigation, transportation and hydroelectric power.
Understand the causes and effects of river pollution.
